label: dioscorides::book1_ch1_pepper
text: De Materia Medica, Pedanius Dioscorides (77 CE), Book I. On Pepper (κόσκινον / Piper nigrum, P. longum). 'Both kinds of pepper are warming, diuretic, and digestive. They break up intestinal gas, are beneficial for those chilled and shivering, clear the eyesight when rubbed on eyelids, attract moisture from the head, and are given for tetanus and epilepsy in wine. Applied with honey they clear corneal film. The long pepper is more warming than the round. Adulterated pepper may be detected by water — true pepper sinks, adulterated floats.' Preparation: taken in wine or honey, applied topically to eyes. Conditions: digestive complaints, chills, tetanus, eye conditions, head congestion.

label: dioscorides::book1_ch6_iris
text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Iris (ἶρις / Iris germanica and related species). 'The root is warming, sharp, and somewhat bitter. It softens and draws out splinters, and being applied with vinegar it disperses hard swellings under the chin. Taken in wine it relieves pain of the sides, convulsions, ruptures, coughs, and inflammation of the trachea. With honey applied to the head it cures dandruff. Applied as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux and expels the afterbirth. The smoke drives away serpents.' Preparation: root in wine or honey internally; applied topically with vinegar or honey. Conditions: gynecological, respiratory, wounds, swellings.

label: dioscorides::book1_ch12_nard
text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Celtic Nard and Indian Nard (Nardus / Nardostachys jatamansi and related). 'Nard is warming and drying, good for the stomach, it stops diarrhea, is diuretic, and applied in oil it is good for the head. It benefits the liver and kidneys, clears jaundice. Given in wine with pepper it helps those bitten by serpents. Applied to the eyes with raisin wine it removes inflammation. The Indian kind is stronger than the Celtic.' Preparation: root in wine or oil; applied topically. Conditions: digestive, liver, kidney, eye inflammation, snakebite.

label: dioscorides::book1_ch24_costus
text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Costus (Κόστος / Saussurea costus). 'Costus is warming and drying. Taken in drink it helps those bitten by serpents and those with pain in the side. It moves the menstrual flux, provokes urine, helps those with dropsy, and eases epilepsy. Taken with wine it helps against poisons. Applied with honey it removes dark blotches and spots. The root burned as incense drives away serpents. The Arabian kind is best, then the Indian.' Preparation: root in wine or honey. Conditions: menstrual disorders, edema, epilepsy, skin blemishes, snakebite.

label: dioscorides::book2_ch76_opium
text: De Materia Medica, Book II. On Poppy Juice/Opium (ὀπός μήκωνος / Papaver somniferum). 'The juice of the wild poppy is the most powerful of the soporifics. It eases pain, induces sleep, aids digestion, is good for coughs and for spitting blood. Taken in drink it benefits those with chronic illnesses and with intestinal pains. But used too much it harms, for it makes men lethargic and kills them. One may know the genuine by its bitterness and by its rapid heaviness upon the senses. The adulterated is less bitter and less effective.' Preparation: dried latex dissolved in wine or water. Conditions: pain, insomnia, cough, intestinal pain. Critical note: Dioscorides explicitly warns of lethal overdose — the first documented pharmacological warning about opioids in Western medicine.

label: dioscorides::book2_ch82_willow
text: De Materia Medica, Book II. On Willow (ἰτέα / Salix species). 'The leaves and bark of the willow, being boiled with a little pepper and drunk, they stop the flux of the belly. The juice pressed from the leaves being dropped into the ears stops the pain in them. The bark burned and dissolved in vinegar removes corns and calluses. The leaves bruised small and taken with a little pepper and wine stop the flowing of the seed in those troubled by dreams. The bark of the willow being boiled and the decoction drunk helps those troubled with gout.' Preparation: leaves boiled in water; bark decocted; leaves bruised. Conditions: diarrhea, ear pain, gout, skin conditions.

label: dioscorides::book3_ch1_wheat
text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Wheat and Grain Medicines (πυρός / Triticum and related). 'The flour of wheat applied in a poultice with boiled figs mollifies and ripens swellings. Bread crumbs chewed and applied draw out splinters. The water in which wheat has been boiled is good for the throat when gargled. The decoction of green wheat applied with oil to the temples eases headache. Old lard rendered with wheat flour applied warm relieves pain of the joints and helps those with gout and sciatica.' Preparation: flour poultice; bread applied; decoctions. Conditions: swellings, splinters, sore throat, headache, joint pain.

label: dioscorides::book3_ch25_crocus
text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Saffron/Crocus (κρόκος / Crocus sativus). 'Saffron has a warming, softening, and sleep-inducing faculty. It benefits the eyes and the stomach. Taken in drink it sharpens the sight. Applied with honey and wine to inflammations it helps them. Taken with wine in drink it cheers the mind. Given with wine it helps those bitten by the land-crocodile. Applied as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux. But taken in excess it harms by inducing a kind of intoxication.' Preparation: stigmas in wine or honey. Conditions: eye conditions, inflammation, depression, menstrual disorders. Note: Dioscorides' observation that saffron 'cheers the mind' is an early record of antidepressant effect — now validated by multiple RCTs.

label: dioscorides::book3_ch81_aloe
text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Aloe (ἀλόη / Aloe vera and related species). 'Aloe has the property of binding and is somewhat bitter. It stops bleeding from wounds when applied, closes together wounds, and is good for the eyes. Taken in drink it loosens the belly and purges bile. Applied to the head with wine and oil it clears dandruff. It stops the falling off of hair. Applied warm it heals the haemorrhoids. The best is the Barbarian kind, which is transparent like glass and saffron-coloured.' Preparation: applied directly to wounds and eyes; taken in drink for internal conditions. Conditions: wounds, eye conditions, constipation, dandruff, hemorrhoids.

label: dioscorides::book4_ch75_mandrake
text: De Materia Medica, Book IV. On Mandrake (μανδραγόρας / Mandragora officinarum). 'The root has the quality of warming. Wine made of the root, a cyathus [small measure] being drunk, is a soporific for those who need to be cut or cauterized, that they may not feel the pain. Given in drink to those with serpent-bites, and in drink for those who drink the juice of aconite [poison antidote]. Applied as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux. The bark and root applied with oil heals inflammation and prurigo. But excess causes lethargy and even death.' Preparation: root in wine as surgical anesthetic; applied topically. Conditions: surgical anesthesia, snakebite, menstrual disorders. Note: earliest documented description of surgical anesthesia in European medicine — 1,800 years before ether.

label: dioscorides::book4_ch138_datura
text: De Materia Medica, Book IV. On Stramonium/Thorn Apple (στράμμωνον / Datura species). 'The root being drunk in wine makes the mind wander — a drachm weight makes one have a vision beyond the ordinary, two drachms bring on madness for three days, four drachms kill. The antidote is pine resin drunk in wine. Applied topically with vinegar it is good for skin diseases and abscesses. The seed hung around the neck as an amulet helps against epilepsy — but this may be superstition.' Preparation: root in wine in precise small doses for vision; topical application. Conditions: surgical anesthesia, vision, epilepsy. Note: Dioscorides documents both the therapeutic and toxic effects of Datura at different doses — a sophisticated dose-response observation.

label: dioscorides::book5_ch2_honey
text: De Materia Medica, Book V. On Honey (μέλι / Apis mellifera product). 'Honey, being boiled and applied to old wounds, draws out what is in them and cleanses them and produces flesh. Taken with water it clears the chest and lungs and loosens the cough. It digests and expels what burdens the stomach. Attic honey is the most praised — it is dry and does not rot. Applied to the teeth and gums it fastens loose teeth. Rubbed on the eyelids it removes the darkness before the eyes. The worst honey is that from box trees and yew — it produces nausea and vomiting.' Preparation: applied directly to wounds; taken with water; rubbed on teeth and eyes. Conditions: wounds, respiratory, digestive, eye conditions, dental.

label: dioscorides::book5_copper_verdigris
text: De Materia Medica, Book V. On Copper / Verdigris (χαλκός / Copper and copper compounds). 'The flower of copper [verdigris] being applied stops the flows of blood from wounds and cleanses foul ulcers. Applied with honey it draws together the edges of wounds. Used as an eye salve it sharpens the sight. Beaten fine and applied it stops excessive menstrual flow. Washed copper applied with vinegar removes eruptions of the skin and dandruff. Copper vessels for water storage keep the water from putrefying.' Preparation: verdigris applied directly to wounds; copper compound eye salves; copper water storage vessels. Conditions: wound infection, eye conditions, skin disease. Note: Dioscorides' recommendation of copper vessels for water storage is an early documentation of the oligodynamic antimicrobial effect — the same mechanism independently discovered by Egyptian medicine and Anglo-Saxon medicine.

label: dioscorides::book1_ch68_garlic
text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Garlic (σκόρδον / Allium sativum). 'Garlic has a sharp and heating faculty. Eaten it provokes urine, is good for the stomach that is cold and full of wind, clears the arteries, is given to those troubled with coughs and with asthma. Applied it draws out leeches and other things that have got into the body. It expels worms and kills lice and nits when applied externally. Given as a pessary it moves the menstrual flux. Applied to the head it kills lice. When eaten it harms those of a warm body temperament. The wild kind is more effective than the cultivated.' Preparation: eaten raw or cooked; applied topically; given as pessary. Conditions: respiratory, parasites, digestive gas, menstrual disorders, lice. Note: Dioscorides' comprehensive garlic entry prefigures modern research on allicin as antimicrobial and on garlic's cardiovascular effects.

label: dioscorides::book1_ch105_valerian
text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Valerian (φοῦ / Valeriana species). 'The root is warming and has a singular quality. Taken in drink it provokes urine and the menstrual flux. It benefits those with pain in the side, and it is good for those bitten by serpents. Some call it nardus silvestris because its smell resembles nard. The roots of the Pontic kind are better than those from other places.' Preparation: root in wine. Conditions: urinary, menstrual, side pain, snakebite. Note: Valerian's documented calming and sedative effect — the primary reason it is now sold globally as a sleep supplement — is not mentioned by Dioscorides, suggesting this application developed later in European folk medicine rather than deriving from the classical tradition.

label: dioscorides::book3_ch49_chamomile
text: De Materia Medica, Book III. On Chamomile (ἀνθεμίς / Matricaria chamomilla / Anthemis nobilis). 'Chamomile being boiled and the decoction taken in a bath softens and eases pain. Taken in drink it helps those with kidney stones and provokes urine. The flowers boiled in oil and the oil poured into the ears ease pain in the ears. Applied in a poultice it ripens and disperses hard swellings. The oil made from the flowers is good for head-ache and for all pains and tightness of the sinews.' Preparation: flowers decocted as bath and drink; flowers in oil for ears and head; poultice. Conditions: kidney stones, ear pain, headache, swellings, muscle pain. Note: Chamomile's use for pain and inflammation by Dioscorides maps directly onto its modern pharmacology — alpha-bisabolol and apigenin are validated anti-inflammatory compounds.

label: dioscorides::book4_ch68_henbane
text: De Materia Medica, Book IV. On Henbane (ὑοσκύαμος / Hyoscyamus niger). 'Henbane causes confusion of mind and sleep. Applied to the forehead and temples with oil of rose it causes sleep. The seed drunk with wine makes men insane and similar to drunkards for a time. The leaves applied in a poultice ease pain of the joints and of gout. Used as a fumigation it is good for pain of the teeth. Applied to swellings and inflammations it reduces them. But used too freely it brings madness and even death.' Preparation: leaves in oil topically; fumigation for dental pain; seed in wine. Conditions: pain, insomnia, gout, dental pain, inflammation. Note: Henbane (hyoscyamine/scopolamine) is in the same family as Datura and Toloache — another cross-tradition convergence, with Aztec (Badianus), Andean (Cobo), and Greek/Roman traditions all independently documenting the same genus for pain relief and the same warning about lethal overdose.

label: dioscorides::book2_ch159_colchicum
text: De Materia Medica, Book II. On Colchicum (κολχικόν / Colchicum autumnale). 'The bulb of kolchikon, being drunk in wine, is good for those who are bitten by scorpions. But taken in excess it strangles like mushrooms. The flower, drunk in wine, makes the stomach griping pains stop — but it is not to be used except for those who are strong and in good health, and even then only in small amounts, for the plant is sharp and harmful.' Preparation: bulb in wine in small doses. Conditions: gout (joint pain from rich diet), scorpion sting. Note: Dioscorides' warning about colchicum being dangerous in excess directly parallels the Islamic tradition's (Ibn Sina's) identical warning — and colchicine remains the gold-standard gout treatment with an identically narrow therapeutic window today.

label: dioscorides::book1_ch93_cassia
text: De Materia Medica, Book I. On Cassia and Cinnamon (κασσία / Cinnamomum cassia, C. verum). 'Cassia and Cinnamon are warming, digestive, and diuretic. Taken in drink they help those bitten by serpents and those with pain in the sides. They move the menstrual flux. They benefit those with cold and moist stomachs. Applied with oil to the face they remove spots and blemishes. Taken in drink with wine they warm those who are cold. Cinnamon is more powerful than cassia in all these uses — it is more warming and more fragrant.' Preparation: bark in wine or water; applied topically with oil. Conditions: cold conditions, digestive, menstrual, snakebite, skin blemishes.

label: dioscorides::book2_ch68_animal_medicines
text: De Materia Medica, Book II. Animal-derived medicines (overview chapter). 'Many medicines are derived from animals. The bile of bears is good for those troubled by dim sight. The gall of the river fish Hyena is drunk for those struck with epilepsy. The blood of the tortoise dropped into the ears helps those with pain in the ears. The fat of geese applied with sulphur and oil helps those with alopecia. The brains of the hare eaten raw cure trembling. Cow dung mixed with old oil applied to the forehead stops headache. The dried liver of a wolf drunk in wine helps those with pain of the stomach and liver.' Note: Dioscorides' animal medicine section is the most extensive in Book II — demonstrating that Greek/Roman medicine was not exclusively botanical but drew heavily on animal pharmacology alongside plant remedies.

label: dioscorides::book5_81_quicksilver
text: De Materia Medica, Book V. On Mercury/Quicksilver (ὑδράργυρος / Mercurius). 'Quicksilver is produced by pounding cinnabar in iron mortars with vinegar. It is very cold and moist in faculty and is deadly to living things, killing by its weight if swallowed. It dissolves gold and silver and for this reason it is kept in lead or glass or silver vessels. It is used in ointments for lice and for mange in animals. Those who work with it have trembling of the hands.' Preparation: topical only. Conditions: lice, mange. Note: Dioscorides recognises mercury toxicity ('deadly to living things') and documents the occupational tremors of mercury workers — the first description of mercury poisoning in European medical literature, predating modern understanding of mercurialism by 1,800 years.
